documents.title: Capacity Building at Copernicus Berlin
documents.text:
Projects
Capacity Building
Copernicus Berlin builds capacity through high-quality international training, non-formal education, and practical project work that helps youth workers, partner organisations, and communities grow stronger over time.
What capacity building means at Copernicus Berlin
For Copernicus Berlin, capacity building is not a side activity. It is a core way of creating long-term impact. The organisation develops people, methods, and structures so that learning does not stop when a project ends.
This work is closely linked to Erasmus+ training activities and to the wider mission of strengthening inclusive, democratic, and sustainable societies through international education, civic engagement, and cooperation.
How we build capacity
Training with practical value
Copernicus Berlin creates learning formats that equip youth workers and partners with methods they can apply directly in real educational, social, and community contexts.
Inclusion and participation
The organisation focuses on approaches that help reach marginalised young people, widen participation, and strengthen meaningful engagement in democratic and community life.
Digital and green transition
Capacity building also means preparing organisations and youth work practices for digital transformation, sustainability, and future-oriented project implementation.
Stronger organisations and partnerships
By connecting training, project cooperation, and shared standards, Copernicus Berlin helps partners improve quality, collaboration, and long-term effectiveness.
Why this matters
Copernicus Berlin presents capacity building as a way to improve quality, relevance, and impact across training activities. The goal is not only to deliver good events, but to create lasting competence, stronger cooperation, and better learning opportunities for the people and organisations involved.
This is one of the reasons the organisation stands out: it combines educational ambition with implementation experience, and it treats training as a serious investment in people, practice, and future cooperation.
Key focus areas
Competence development for youth workers and project teams
Inclusion, diversity, and access for participants with fewer opportunities
Environmental sustainability and responsible project design
Digital transformation and modern non-formal learning methods
Civic engagement, employability, and long-term community impact
Stronger links between policy, research, and practical youth work

documents.title: Femme Financial Freedom Project
documents.text:
Erasmus+
Key Action 2
FEMME FINANCIAL FREEDOM
The project aims to boost financial literacy among young migrant and refugee women, supporting their economic independence and integration through a tailored online course and a toolkit for youth workers.
Description
The Femme Financial Freedom (3F) project, coordinated by Copernicus Berlin and implemented in partnership with organizations from Spain, Bulgaria, and Romania, aims to empower young migrant and refugee women by enhancing their financial literacy. Running from September 2023 to May 2025, the project responds to the increasing feminisation of migration and the associated social and economic challenges faced by women newcomers in Europe. Many of these women struggle with financial independence due to low financial literacy, cultural and linguistic barriers, and limited access to relevant information and support systems.
To address these issues, the project will develop an innovative online training course tailored to the needs of young migrant and refugee women, equipping them with essential financial knowledge and skills to foster their economic inclusion and personal empowerment. The initiative unfolds in two phases: first, the development of the course and its hosting platform, and second, its assessment through a pilot mobility activity followed by the refinement of materials and the creation of a Financial Literacy Inclusion Toolkit for youth workers. This toolkit will help professionals better support young women in overcoming economic barriers to integration.
The final months of the project focus on widespread dissemination and sustainability of results through awareness campaigns, policy recommendations, and national conferences in each partner country. The project ultimately seeks not only to provide direct support to its primary target group but also to generate lasting resources for youth workers and policymakers, ensuring long-term impact and broader uptake of its educational tools.
Results
Learning-outcomes report: This 16-page document sets out eleven clear learning outcomes that guided the whole 3F project. It explains the basic financial knowledge, skills and attitudes young migrant and refugee women need in order to become economically independent. Download the report here: /external-files/copernicusberlin.org__3F_Report_.pdf
Online training programme and platform: An open, ten-module course (English, Bulgarian, German and Romanian) that covers budgeting, credit, digital banking, investment basics, entrepreneurship and adaptation to EU financial systems. Participants work at their own pace and receive a certificate on completion. Start the course here: https://femme-financial-freedom.eu/platform/.
The pilot mobility in Bulgaria was a seven-day residential test of the course with twenty young migrant and refugee women drawn from the four partner countries. Participants went through every unit, offered detailed feedback and took part in focus-group interviews; their comments led to the final refinements of the training materials.
Financial-literacy inclusion toolkit: An 80-page handbook for youth workers. It offers background on financial-inclusion barriers, step-by-step non-formal activities, ready-made worksheets, digital-tool suggestions and real case studies. Download the toolkit here: EN /external-files/copernicusberlin.org__Toolkit_EN.pdf, ES /external-files/copernicusberlin.org__Toolkit_ES.pdf, BG /external-files/copernicusberlin.org__Toolkit_BG.pdf, DE /external-files/copernicusberlin.org__Toolkit_DE.pdf, RO /external-files/copernicusberlin.org__Toolkit_RO.pdf
Policy-recommendation paper: A concise set of proposals addressed to local and national authorities on how to improve financial inclusion for young migrant and refugee women. It draws on project evidence and lists practical measures for education, banking and labour-market actors. Read the paper here: EN /external-files/copernicusberlin.org__W4_Policy-Recommendations_EN.pdf, ES /external-files/copernicusberlin.org__WP4-Policy-Recommendations_ES.pdf, BG /external-files/copernicusberlin.org__W4_Policy-Recommendations_BG.pdf, DE /external-files/copernicusberlin.org__W4_Policy-Recommendations_DE.pdf, RO /external-files/copernicusberlin.org__W4_Policy-Recommendations_RO.pdf
The final conferences were one-day events held in Bulgaria, Spain, Germany and Romania at the close of the project. Each gathered around thirty stakeholders, including youth organisations, social entrepreneurs and women with minority and refugee backgrounds, to present results, showcase success stories, and discuss future cooperation and policy uptake.
Project ID: 2023-1-DE04-KA220-YOU-000151083
The project aims to boost financial literacy among young migrant and refugee women, supporting their economic independence and integration through a tailored online course and a toolkit for youth workers.

documents.title: G2C Project at Copernicus Berlin
documents.text:
Erasmus+
Key Action 2
GIVE THEM A 2 CHANCE (G2C)
The project aims to support the development of second-chance entrepreneurship attitude and initiative and provide a path for entrepreneurs whose first businesses have failed or been not successful to re-enter the market.
Summary of the project
SMEs have been the lifeblood of the European economy, accounting for more than two-thirds of the workforce and more than half of the economic value added (Eurostat). Yet the results of a McKinsey survey conducted in August 2020, of more than 2,200 SMEs in France, Germany, Italy, Spain and the United Kingdom, indicate how hard their prosperity has been hit by the COVID-19 crisis, and more than half of those surveyed worried they might not be in business in 12 months. Even before COVID, every year in the EU around 200,000 firms went bankrupt, resulting in over 1.7 million people losing their jobs (EU Directorate-General for Justice and Consumers). Encouraging continuing entrepreneurship, even after failure not linked to dishonesty, is therefore essential to the health of Member States' economies.
Many ex-entrepreneurs would like to create a new business, but they face numerous obstacles in doing so. They feel discouraged to re-start due to the stigma of failure and due to practical difficulties or discrimination after bankruptcy. Failure also has a secondary effect: many would-be entrepreneurs do not start a company because of fear of the consequences of business failure.
This has negative effects on growth and employment in Europe. While EU policies supporting entrepreneurship already include references to second chance approaches, this concept is still almost absent from VET provision. VET providers often offer the same services to failed and first-time entrepreneurs, although their situations require different approaches.
Therefore, Give Them a 2 Chance (G2C) supports the development of second-chance entrepreneurial attitude and initiative and provides a path for entrepreneurs whose first businesses failed, or were not successful, to re-enter the market.
G2C objectives include: encouraging failed and ex-entrepreneurs to learn from past experience through tools that help them understand reasons behind failure, entrepreneurial potential and attitude; supporting them in rebuilding competences for a successful business through digital learning modules and an online platform; equipping VET teachers, trainers and mentors with educational resources for mentoring failed and ex-entrepreneurs; and promoting SME support policies that facilitate return to business.
G2C brings together partners active in VET and enterprise education such as SMEs, NGOs and chambers of commerce committed to providing a positive and supportive environment for second-chance entrepreneurship.
The two main target groups are failed entrepreneurs and ex-entrepreneurs, and mentors, teachers, trainers and educators in VET centres, business associations, chambers of commerce, vocational schools and NGOs working on VET and entrepreneurial education.
Project ID: 2021-1-DE02-KA220-VET-000035651
The project aims to support the development of second-chance entrepreneurship attitude and initiative and provide a path for entrepreneurs whose first businesses have failed or been not successful to re-enter the market.

documents.title: DACG Project at Copernicus Berlin
documents.text:
Erasmus+
Key Action 2
DIFFERENT APPROACHES, ONE COMMON GOAL (DACG)
The project aims to provide educators with innovative tools and competences to develop competences and enhance students' knowledge by using an integrated learning approach.
Summary of the project
Preserve peace, prevent conflicts and strengthen international security (Article 21(2), Lisbon Treaty) is one of the EU's core values. The Global Strategy for the European Union's Foreign and Security Policy establishes clear links between education, conflict and peace. Higher education is not exempt.
Education plays a key role in peacebuilding: learners not only gain academic knowledge, but also moral values, norms, proper conduct and effective communication skills. Peacebuilding education helps students resolve conflicts effectively without aggression, become more engaged citizens, and think critically about links between local and global issues.
However, current educational provision is often fragmented and disconnected. Students lack clear guidance on which competencies they should build and how to combine learning opportunities strategically. Those interested in peacebuilding can learn through formal providers such as universities and non-formal providers such as NGOs, but collaboration between them is often difficult due to differences in pedagogy, ethos and methodology.
As a result, formal and non-formal providers remain disconnected, with a major gap between them.
DACG addresses this by providing educators with innovative tools and competences to strengthen student learning through an integrated approach that encourages questioning, reflection and ethical decision-making grounded in peacebuilding values.
The project integrates formal and non-formal methods and partner experience, treating both as part of lifelong learning. Formal education is often criticised for low interactivity, while non-formal methods promote active participation and learner engagement in building knowledge.
Combining both approaches is therefore vital for a complete cognitive experience for learners.
Project ID: 2021-1-DE01-KA220-HED-000030321
The project aims to provide educators with innovative tools and competences to develop competences and enhance students' knowledge by using an integrated learning approach.

documents.title: DCM Project at Copernicus Berlin
documents.text:
Erasmus+
Key Action 2
DIGITAL CREATIVE MIND
The project aims to create a sustainable and efficient education program dedicated to adult learners with low digital skills involved in the creative cultural sector.
Summary of the project
The cultural and creative sectors (CCS), crucial for the European economy and citizens' well-being, were profoundly affected by measures to fight COVID-19. Artists, creators and cultural operators were severely impacted by postponements, cancellations and closures of events, performances, exhibitions, museums and cultural institutions.
Cultural employment is also precarious by nature: across the EU, almost one third (32 percent) of the cultural workforce was self-employed in 2019, compared with an average of 14 percent for the whole economy (Eurostat, 2020). Many independent and freelance artists do not benefit from full social protection and unemployment schemes.
At the same time, demand for digital cultural content and online sales of cultural goods and services has grown. Cultural institutions and individual artists increasingly moved productions online. The key question is whether workers in the sector have the capacity to digitalise their activity, and whether digital inequalities in access, equipment and skills will deepen social and professional inequalities.
A digital shift is strongly affecting the sector, while digital skills are often concentrated among younger workers. Since 57.2 percent of the cultural workforce is over 40 (Eurostat, 2020), a large share of adults needs support. Mass digitisation and emerging technologies such as virtual and augmented reality can create new forms of cultural experience and business models that adults in CCS can use.
Digital Creative Minds (DCM) therefore develops a sustainable and effective education program for adult learners with low digital skills in CCS. It raises awareness of digital upskilling needs and focuses on openness and inclusion.
DCM develops an open online course on Digital Skills and Social Inclusion for CCS as a MOOC, available in English and partner languages as Open Educational Resource (OER), accessible for adults with limited digital skills.
Project outputs include: Digital Competences for CCS guidelines, online platform, MOOC courses, and a Position Paper on training investments and OERs for raising digital skills in CCS.
The project is integrated into partner organisations' educational activities and improves quality of adult education services. Adult educators and organisations gain specialised knowledge on supporting adult learners and strengthening digital competence in CCS.
Adult learners use the MOOC courses as self-regulated OER learning pathways. This increases digital skills and supports better outcomes for adults in CCS from vulnerable socio-economic groups.
Project ID: 2020-1-DE02-KA227-ADU-007933
The project aims to create a sustainable and efficient education program dedicated to adult learners with low digital skills involved in the creative cultural sector.
info@digitalcreativeminds.eu

documents.title: PTE Project at Copernicus Berlin
documents.text:
Erasmus+
Key Action 2
PATHWAY TO EQUALITY
The project aims to provide adult educators with new skills, knowledge and tools in the field of minority adults' human rights education.
Summary of the project
Pathway to Equality (PTE) is a 20-month Strategic Partnership focused on promoting high-quality adult work by providing adult educators with skills, knowledge and tools in the field of minority adults' human rights education.
The project supports adult educators in developing core human-rights-education competences and in taking action to defend the rights of minority adults, reducing discrimination in society.
The EU recognises minority rights as essential in multicultural societies and acknowledges that minority communities contribute significantly to social richness and diversity. States that recognise and promote minority rights are more likely to remain tolerant and stable.
The consortium therefore sees a strong need to develop competences of adult educators in minority human rights education, both for implementing educational activities and for mainstreaming this perspective.
PTE provides Open Educational Resources (OERs), expanding the range of learning materials available to adult educators and supporting constant curriculum innovation and competence development.
The project contributes to the advancement and recognition of adult work in Europe. It strengthens the relevance of adult education in the field of minority human rights and supports educators in becoming active agents of change and social cohesion.
Adult educators are key actors in formal and non-formal systems, especially in contexts where ethnicity, race or religion challenge social cohesion. PTE provides free educational tools and develops trained educators who contribute to human-rights-oriented adult communities and long-term equality culture.
PTE also empowers disadvantaged adults as direct beneficiaries. One core goal is a Europe where adults with minority backgrounds can exercise their rights without discrimination. Partners strengthen adult-led rights initiatives, support safe dialogue spaces, and bring adults' voices to local, national and European policy levels.
Adult educators in PTE will gain new knowledge and skills in minority human rights, reinforce practical methods and tools, increase motivation and implementation capacity through training events and outputs, expand international networks, improve their ability to respond to new situations, act as local multipliers for Erasmus+ opportunities, and support participants in accessing future learning, mobility and volunteering pathways.
Project ID: 2020-1-DE02-KA204-007595
The project aims to provide adult educators with new skills, knowledge and tools in the field of minority adults' human rights education.
info@pathwaytoequality.eu

documents.title: SYP Project at Copernicus Berlin
documents.text:
Erasmus+
Key Action 2
SHARE YOUR PEACE
The project aims to strengthen the peacebuilding skills of youth workers and provide them with the necessary tools as learning resources to empower young people to actively engage in peaceful change.
Summary of the project
For centuries, Europe was a continent of war. After the Second World War, the European Union became a successful peace project and was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 2012. Today, peacebuilding and conflict prevention remain central to EU action.
The EU seeks to promote peace, its values and citizens' well-being, and to preserve peace, prevent conflicts and strengthen international security. In recent years, however, this role has been challenged by violent and complex conflicts such as in Syria, Libya and Ukraine, whose consequences remain severe.
These conflicts and related refugee and migration dynamics have increased social tension and insecurity perceptions, creating fertile ground for xenophobic and populist reactions.
To address these challenges, the EU has promoted youth participation in peacebuilding and strengthened Youth, Peace and Security policy. Young people's leadership in preventing and resolving conflict is a key resource for sustainable peace.
Yet enabling meaningful youth participation requires social approaches that connect young people with civil society and provide opportunities, training and support. Youth work can play a major role in developing this potential.
Before applying, consortium partners, including youth organisations from Georgia and Ukraine, conducted needs analysis in each country through focus groups and online questionnaires among youth workers and youth leaders.
Findings confirmed a broad need to update youth workers' knowledge and competences in peacebuilding, alongside a clear lack of learning resources and training materials in this field.
Share Your Peace therefore strengthens youth workers' competences in peace education and provides practical tools to help young people become proactive agents of peaceful change through local community initiatives addressing societal conflict.
The direct target group is youth workers involved in youth learning; the indirect target group is young people who benefit from higher-quality youth learning.
The project activities include four transnational meetings, four intellectual outputs, four multiplier events, one short-term joint staff training mobility, and local dissemination actions.
The project creates direct positive effects on participants by improving specialised peace-education knowledge, strengthening peacebuilding and conflict-prevention competences, enabling peer learning and intercultural exchange, and improving professional growth and recognition of youth educational work by stakeholders.
Project ID: 2020-1-DE04-KA205-019057
The project aims to strengthen the peacebuilding skills of youth workers and provide them with the necessary tools as learning resources to empower young people to actively engage in peaceful change.
info@ka2shareyourpeace.eu

documents.title: Career and Participation Opportunities
documents.text:
Career
Join Our Team
Exciting professional journey filled with growth opportunities, challenges and a supportive, inclusive and international team. We are more than just a team, we are a family.
Career Paths
Volunteering
Contribute your skills, gain practical experience, and grow in an international social-impact environment.
Read more
>
Job Vacancy
Explore current openings and join the team to shape impactful educational and professional initiatives.
Read more
>
Why Join Us
1. Impactful Work: At COPERNICUS BERLIN, you will be part of a team that works on projects with real-world significance. Your efforts will directly contribute to a more sustainable and resilient future.
2. Innovation: We foster a culture of innovation and encourage team members to think outside the box. You will have the opportunity to work with the latest technologies and methodologies, pushing the boundaries of what is possible.
3. Collaboration: Collaboration is at the heart of our success. You will work alongside a diverse group of professionals who are passionate about their work and eager to share their knowledge.
4. Learning and Development: We are committed to helping you grow both personally and professionally. We provide continuous learning opportunities, training programs, and mentorship to support your career development.
Our Working Culture
COPERNICUS BERLIN is not just a workplace; it is a community of individuals dedicated to a common purpose - advancing equal education and fostering professional growth. Our organizational culture is rooted in principles that reflect our mission, values, and commitment to making a positive impact in the world.
1. Inclusivity and Diversity: We believe in the strength of diversity and inclusivity. Our team is comprised of individuals from diverse backgrounds, experiences, and perspectives. We understand that by embracing differences, we can create a richer and more innovative environment where everyone's voice is valued and heard.
2. Equal Education: Education is at the core of our mission. We are passionate about creating opportunities for underprivileged individuals to access quality education. Our culture is driven by the belief that education is a fundamental right, not a privilege, and we work tirelessly to remove barriers that hinder access to learning.
3. Empowerment: We empower our team members to take ownership of their roles and initiatives. We encourage creativity and provide the freedom to explore innovative solutions to complex challenges. Our culture of empowerment extends to the communities we serve, as we aim to empower individuals through education to improve their own lives and communities.
4. Professional Growth: We are committed to the growth and development of our team members. We provide ongoing training, mentorship, and opportunities for skill enhancement. Our culture values continuous learning and recognizes that when our team members grow professionally, they can better contribute to our mission.
5. Collaboration and Teamwork: Collaboration is at the heart of our culture. We believe that by working together, we can achieve more significant impact. Our teams collaborate across departments, share knowledge, and support one another in achieving our common goals.
6. Transparency and Accountability: We operate with transparency and accountability in all our actions. We are responsible stewards of the resources entrusted to us, ensuring that every amount invested in our organization goes towards furthering our mission of equal education and professional growth.
7. Passion and Commitment: Passion for our mission is what drives us forward. Our team is united by a shared commitment to making a difference in the lives of those we serve. This shared dedication creates a culture where each individual's efforts contribute to a larger collective impact.
Professional CV Template
Copernicus Berlin has created a professional CV template, complete with detailed recommendations to streamline the process of crafting your CV. This template is designed to make creating your CV easy and understandable, ensuring you include all the essential information in a clear and organized manner. Most importantly, using this template will significantly increase your chances of being invited for an interview by highlighting your skills, experience, and qualifications in a compelling way. Whether you are just starting your career or looking to advance to the next level, this CV template will help you stand out in a competitive job market and make a strong first impression on potential employers.

documents.title: Volunteering at Copernicus Berlin
documents.text:
Career
Volunteering
Drive Impact
Dive into meaningful volunteering, create lasting impact, and become part of a community dedicated to positive change.
Power of COPERNICUS BERLIN Volunteering
Volunteering is a powerful avenue for personal and professional development that often goes underestimated. Beyond the intrinsic rewards of helping COPERNICUS BERLIN and making a positive impact on your community, volunteering can be a catalyst for your significant professional growth.
Skill Development
Volunteering with COPERNICUS BERLIN offers an excellent opportunity to acquire and refine valuable skills that are relevant to your professional life. You can gain hands-on experience that can be applied to your career. These skills may include:
Leadership: Take on leadership roles within volunteer projects, honing your ability to manage teams, set goals, and drive results.
Communication: Enhance your communication skills by interacting with diverse groups of people and effectively conveying your ideas.
Problem Solving: Tackle real-world challenges and develop your problem-solving abilities in a supportive environment.
Time Management: Learn to balance your volunteer commitments with your regular work, demonstrating excellent time management skills.
Project Management: Gain experience in planning, executing, and evaluating projects, which can be transferable to your professional life.
Networking Opportunities
COPERNICUS BERLIN Volunteering introduces you to a wide range of individuals who share your passion for a cause or industry. These connections can prove invaluable in your professional journey. Here is how volunteering can help you expand your network:
Meet like-minded professionals: Connect with individuals who have similar interests and career aspirations, fostering meaningful relationships.
Access mentors and advisors: Experienced volunteers and professionals may offer guidance, advice, and mentorship opportunities.
Enhance your online presence: COPERNICUS BERLIN has an online presence, allowing you to showcase your skills and dedication, which can attract potential employers or collaborators.
CV Enhancement
Volunteering with an international organization like Copernicus Berlin adds depth and breadth to your CV, making you a more attractive candidate to potential employers. It demonstrates your commitment, adaptability, and your ability to contribute beyond your job description. When employers see volunteer experience on your resume, they may interpret it as:
A strong work ethic: Volunteering showcases your willingness to go above and beyond.
A commitment to personal growth: It indicates that you actively seek opportunities to improve yourself.
A passion for community and social responsibility: Employers value candidates who care about giving back.
Building Soft Skills
In addition to technical skills, volunteering with COPERNICUS BERLIN can help you develop critical soft skills that are highly sought after in the professional world. These skills include empathy, teamwork, adaptability, and cultural competence. They can make you a more effective and well-rounded professional, capable of thriving in diverse work environments.
How to Get Involved
Volunteering with us is a fulfilling and rewarding experience, allowing you to contribute to our mission of creating a sustainable world.
Prepare Your CV
To get started, please prepare your complete chronological curriculum vitae (CV). Your CV should outline your educational background, work experience, and any relevant skills or qualifications.
You can create perfect CV with our online CV-builder.
Specify Your Availability
When you send your CV, make sure to indicate the estimated duration of your volunteering commitment. This helps us tailor your volunteering experience to your availability and our current needs.
Choose Your Start Date
Let us know when you would like to begin your volunteering journey with Copernicus Berlin. We will do our best to accommodate your preferred start date.
Email Us
Complete your application on the CAMPUS platform. Our team will review your application and respond as soon as possible.
CAMPUS platform
What Awaits You
As a volunteer with Copernicus Berlin, you can look forward to a wide array of exciting experiences and opportunities that will enrich both your personal and professional life. Join our team and discover what awaits you:
Comfortable Accommodation in the Heart of Berlin:
You will have the chance to live in a comfortable apartment located within the vibrant campus of Copernicus Berlin, right in the city center. Enjoy a convenient and cozy living space while being at the heart of the action.
Participation in International Events:
You will have the opportunity to participate in a wide range of international events hosted by Copernicus Berlin. Engage with a diverse and dynamic community, gain valuable insights, and broaden your global perspective.
Exciting Travel Opportunities:
Embark on incredible journeys as you travel to various countries, representing Copernicus Berlin in international initiatives and collaborations. Explore new cultures, meet people from around the world, and expand your horizons.
Personal and Professional Skills Development:
Copernicus Berlin is committed to your growth. Throughout your volunteer experience, you will have the chance to develop both your personal and professional skills, enhancing your capabilities and boosting your self-confidence.
Extensive Networking Opportunities:
Connect with like-minded individuals, professionals, and experts from various fields. Your time as a volunteer will allow you to build an extensive network that can open doors to future opportunities and collaborations.
CV Enhancement:
Your involvement with Copernicus Berlin will be a significant addition to your resume. Showcase your dedication to social causes, your international exposure, and your ability to work in diverse teams to potential employers and institutions.
Soft Skills Building and Improvement:
Enhance your soft skills, such as communication, teamwork, leadership, and problem-solving, through hands-on experiences and mentorship. These skills are invaluable for personal and professional growth.
Certificate of Completion and Achievement:
Upon successful completion of your volunteer work, you will receive a valuable certificate that acknowledges your dedication and contributions. This certificate will serve as a testament to your commitment and skills development during your time with us.
Submit application through CAMPUS

documents.title: Social Hub and Community Networking
documents.text:
Career
Social Hub
Growing together
Copernicus Berlin's Social Hub program offers comprehensive support and consulting services aimed at fostering the development and success of new associations and NGOs, as well as enhancing the operations of established organizations.
For New Associations and NGOs:
You want to establish a new NGO but do not know where to start or how to handle operational tasks. By becoming part of the Social Hub program, our experts will share their expertise and support you in creating a stable organization.
Consultation Services
Organizational Setup: Guidance on legal requirements, structure, and registration processes for establishing a new association or NGO.
Mission and Vision Development: Assistance in defining the organization's mission, vision, and core values.
Strategic Planning: Support in creating strategic plans that outline goals, objectives, and actionable steps for the future.
Support Services
Funding Opportunities: Identification of potential funding sources and assistance with grant applications.
Networking: Facilitating connections with other NGOs, associations, and relevant stakeholders to build a strong support network.
Resource Allocation: Advice on effective allocation of resources to maximize impact.
Guidance Services
Compliance and Governance: Ensuring adherence to regulatory requirements and best practices in governance.
Operational Efficiency: Tips and tools for efficient management and operational processes.
For Established Associations and NGOs:
You want to improve your NGO, receive new grants, or explore the Erasmus+ world but do not know where to start or how to write projects. By joining the Social Hub program, you gain a unique opportunity to access a network of experienced organizations and their know-how, as well as experts who will support the development of your NGO.
Management Consulting
Leadership Development: Training and workshops to enhance leadership skills within the organization.
Performance Improvement: Assessment and recommendations for improving organizational performance and impact.
Financial Management: Guidance on budgeting, financial planning, and sustainable financial practices.
Erasmus+ Project Designing and Writing
Proposal Development: Assistance in writing successful Erasmus+ project proposals, including project design, budgeting, and documentation.
Partnership Building: Support in finding and securing partnerships with other organizations across Europe.
Project Management: Guidance on managing Erasmus+ projects effectively from initiation to completion.
Event, Conference, and Training Organization
Event Planning: Comprehensive planning and execution of events, including logistics, marketing, and participant engagement.
Conference Management: Organizing conferences that foster knowledge exchange, networking, and collaboration among stakeholders.
Training Programs: Designing and delivering training programs tailored to the specific needs of the organization, covering areas such as leadership, project management, and sector-specific skills.
Developing Corporate Identity
Branding: Creating a strong brand identity, including logo design, color schemes, and visual guidelines.
Marketing Materials: Designing professional marketing materials such as brochures, websites, and social media profiles.
Public Relations: Strategies for effective communication with the media and the public to build a positive reputation.
How to Contact Us for Services
If you are interested in creating a new association or want to increase your efficiency as an existing NGO but are not aware of operational nuances or tired of bureaucracy, you are welcome to get in touch with us. Here's how:
Arrange a Call
Write an email to info@copernicusberlin.de or send a message to our WhatsApp at +49 176 6242 8816. In your message, indicate that you want to be part of the Social Hub program and specify the services you are interested in.
Sign Agreement
After discussing all the details, an agreement will be signed. You will then become part of the Social Hub program.

documents.title: Privacy Policy and Data Protection
documents.text:
PAGE INFO
Privacy Policy
Data protection declaration
Privacy Policy
Preamble
With the following privacy policy we would like to inform you which types of your personal data (hereinafter also abbreviated as “data”) we process for which purposes and in which scope. The privacy statement applies to all processing of personal data carried out by us, both in the context of providing our services and in particular on our websites, in mobile applications and within external online presences, such as our social media profiles (hereinafter collectively referred to as “online services”).
The terms used are not gender-specific.
Last Update: 8. February 2022
Controller
Copernicus Berlin e.V.
Sophien str. 28/29
10178 Berlin, Germany
Authorised Representatives:
Davit Budaghyan
E-mail address:
info@copernicusberlin.de
Legal Notice:
https://www.copernicusberlin.org/impressum
Contact information of the data protection officer
Aleksandr Rasskazchikov
info@copernicusberlin.de
Overview of processing operations
The following table summarises the types of data processed, the purposes for which they are processed and the concerned data subjects.
Categories of Processed Data
Inventory data.
Payment Data.
Contact data.
Content data.
Contract data.
Usage data.
Meta/communication data.
Job applicant details.
Event Data (Facebook).
Categories of Data Subjects
Customers.
Employees.
Prospective customers.
Communication partner.
Users.
Job applicants.
Members.
Business and contractual partners.
Purposes of Processing
Provision of contractual services and customer support.
Contact requests and communication.
Security measures.
Direct marketing.
Web Analytics.
Office and organisational procedures.
Conversion tracking.
Managing and responding to inquiries.
Job Application Process.
Feedback.
Marketing.
Profiles with user-related information.
Provision of our online services and usability.
Legal Bases for the Processing
In the following, you will find an overview of the legal basis of the GDPR on which we base the processing of personal data. Please note that in addition to the provisions of the GDPR, national data protection provisions of your or our country of residence or domicile may apply. If, in addition, more specific legal bases are applicable in individual cases, we will inform you of these in the data protection declaration.
Consent (Article 6 (1) (a) GDPR)
– The data subject has given consent to the processing of his or her personal data for one or more specific purposes.
Performance of a contract and prior requests (Article 6 (1) (b) GDPR)
– Performance of a contract to which the data subject is party or in order to take steps at the request of the data subject prior to entering into a contract.
Compliance with a legal obligation (Article 6 (1) (c) GDPR)
– Processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation to which the controller is subject.
Legitimate Interests (Article 6 (1) (f) GDPR)
– Processing is necessary for the purposes of the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party, except where such interests are overridden by the interests or fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject which require protection of personal data.
Job application process as a pre-contractual or contractual relationship (Article 9 (2)(b) GDPR)
– If special categories of personal data within the meaning of Article 9 (1) GDPR (e.g. health data, such as severely handicapped status or ethnic origin) are requested from applicants within the framework of the application procedure, so that the responsible person or the person concerned can carry out the obligations and exercising specific rights of the controller or of the data subject in the field of employment and social security and social protection law, their processing shall be carried out in accordance with Article 9 (2)(b) GDPR , in the case of the protection of vital interests of applicants or other persons on the basis of Article 9 (2)(c) GDPR or for the purposes of preventive health care or occupational medicine, for the assessment of the employee’s ability to work, for medical diagnostics, care or treatment in the health or social sector or for the administration of systems and services in the health or social sector in accordance with Article 9 (2)(d) GDPR. In the case of a communication of special categories of data based on voluntary consent, their processing is carried out on the basis of Article 9 (2)(a) GDPR.
In addition to the data protection regulations of the General Data Protection Regulation, national regulations apply to data protection in Germany. This includes in particular the Law on Protection against Misuse of Personal Data in Data Processing (Federal Data Protection Act – BDSG). In particular, the BDSG contains special provisions on the right to access, the right to erase, the right to object, the processing of special categories of personal data, processing for other purposes and transmission as well as automated individual decision-making, including profiling. Furthermore, it regulates data processing for the purposes of the employment relationship (§ 26 BDSG), in particular with regard to the establishment, execution or termination of employment relationships as well as the consent of employees. Furthermore, data protection laws of the individual federal states may apply.
Security Precautions
We take appropriate technical and organisational measures in accordance with the legal requirements, taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, in order to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk.
The measures include, in particular, safeguarding the confidentiality, integrity and availability of data by controlling physical and electronic access to the data as well as access to, input, transmission, securing and separation of the data. In addition, we have established procedures to ensure that data subjects’ rights are respected, that data is erased, and that we are prepared to respond to data threats rapidly. Furthermore, we take the protection of personal data into account as early as the development or selection of hardware, software and service providers, in accordance with the principle of privacy by design and privacy by default.
SSL encryption (https): In order to protect your data transmitted via our online services in the best possible way, we use SSL encryption. You can recognize such encrypted connections by the prefix https:// in the address bar of your browser.
Transmission of Personal Data
In the context of our processing of personal data, it may happen that the data is transferred to other places, companies or persons or that it is disclosed to them. Recipients of this data may include, for example, service providers commissioned with IT tasks or providers of services and content that are embedded in a website. In such a case, the legal requirements will be respected and in particular corresponding contracts or agreements, which serve the protection of your data, will be concluded with the recipients of your data.
Data Transfer within the Organization: We may transfer or otherwise provide access to personal information to other locations within our organization. Insofar as this disclosure is for administrative purposes, the disclosure of the data is based on our legitimate business and economic interests or otherwise, if it is necessary to fulfill our contractual obligations or if the consent of those concerned or otherwise a legal permission is present.
Data Processing in Third Countries
If we process data in a third country (i.e. outside the European Union (EU), the European Economic Area (EEA)) or the processing takes place in the context of the use of third party services or disclosure or transfer of data to other persons, bodies or companies, this will only take place in accordance with the legal requirements.
Subject to express consent or transfer required by contract or law, we process or have processed the data only in third countries with a recognised level of data protection, on the basis of special guarantees, such as a contractual obligation through so-called standard protection clauses of the EU Commission or if certifications or binding internal data protection regulations justify the processing (Article 44 to 49 GDPR, information page of the EU Commission:
https://ec.europa.eu/info/law/law-topic/data-protection/international-dimension-data-protection_en
).
Use of Cookies
Cookies are small text files or other data records that store information on end devices and read information from the end devices. For example, to store the login status in a user account, the contents of a shopping cart in an e-shop, the contents accessed or the functions used. Cookies can also be used for various purposes, e.g. for purposes of functionality, security and convenience of online offers as well as the creation of analyses of visitor flows.
Information on consent:
We use cookies in accordance with the statutory provisions. Therefore, we obtain prior consent from users, except when it is not required by law. In particular, consent is not required if the storage and reading of information, including cookies, is strictly necessary in order to provide an information society service explicitly requested by the subscriber or user. The revocable consent will be clearly communicated to the user and will contain the information on the respective cookie use.
Information on legal bases under data protection law:
The legal basis under data protection law on which we process users’ personal data with the use of cookies depends on whether we ask users for consent. If users consent, the legal basis for processing their data is their declared consent. Otherwise, the data processed with the help of cookies is processed on the basis of our legitimate interests (e.g. in a business operation of our online services and improvement of its usability) or, if this is done in the context of the fulfillment of our contractual obligations, if the use of cookies is necessary to fulfill our contractual obligations. For which purposes the cookies are processed by us, we do clarify in the course of this privacy policy or in the context of our consent and processing procedures.
Retention period:
With regard to the retention period, a distinction is drawn between the following types of cookies:
Temporary cookies (also known as “session cookies”):
Temporary cookies are deleted at the latest after a user has left an online service and closed his or her end device (i.e. browser or mobile application).
Permanent cookies
: Permanent cookies remain stored even after the terminal device is closed. For example, the login status can be saved, or preferred content can be displayed directly when the user visits a website again. Likewise, user data collected with the help of cookies can be used for reach measurement. Unless we provide users with explicit information about the type and storage duration of cookies (e.g., as part of obtaining consent), users should assume that cookies are permanent and that the storage period can be up to two years.
General information on revocation and objection (opt-out):
Users can revoke the consent they have given at any time and also file an objection to processing in accordance with the legal requirements in Art. 21 DSGVO (further information on objection is provided as part of this privacy policy).  Users can also declare their objection by means of the settings of their browser, e.g. by deactivating the use of cookies (whereby this may also limit the functionality of our online services). An objection to the use of cookies for online marketing purposes, can also be declared via the websites
https://optout.aboutads.info
and
https://www.youronlinechoices.com/.
In addition, users can receive further objection notices from us at appropriate points as part of the information on the service providers and cookies used.
Changes and Updates to the Privacy Policy
We kindly ask you to inform yourself regularly about the contents of our data protection declaration. We will adjust the privacy policy as changes in our data processing practices make this necessary. We will inform you as soon as the changes require your cooperation (e.g. consent) or other individual notification.
If we provide addresses and contact information of companies and organizations in this privacy policy, we ask you to note that addresses may change over time and to verify the information before contacting us.
Rights of Data Subjects
As data subject, you are entitled to various rights under the GDPR, which arise in particular from Articles 15 to 21 of the GDPR:
Right to Object: You have the right, on grounds arising from your particular situation, to object at any time to the processing of your personal data which is based on letter (e) or (f) of Article 6(1) GDPR, including profiling based on those provisions. Where personal data are processed for direct marketing purposes, you have the right to object at any time to the processing of the personal data concerning you for the purpose of such marketing, which includes profiling to the extent that it is related to such direct marketing.
Right of withdrawal for consents:
You have the right to revoke consents at any time.
Right of access:
You have the right to request confirmation as to whether the data in question will be processed and to be informed of this data and to receive further information and a copy of the data in accordance with the provisions of the law.
Right to rectification:
You have the right, in accordance with the law, to request the completion of the data concerning you or the rectification of the incorrect data concerning you.
Right to Erasure and Right to Restriction of Processing:
In accordance with the statutory provisions, you have the right to demand that the relevant data be erased immediately or, alternatively, to demand that the processing of the data be restricted in accordance with the statutory provisions.
Right to data portability:
You have the right to receive data concerning you which you have provided to us in a structured, common and machine-readable format in accordance with the legal requirements, or to request its transmission to another controller.
Complaint to the supervisory authority:
In accordance with the law and without prejudice to any other administrative or judicial remedy, you also have the right to lodge a complaint with a data protection supervisory authority, in particular a supervisory authority in the Member State where you habitually reside, the supervisory authority of your place of work or the place of the alleged infringement, if you consider that the processing of personal data concerning you infringes the GDPR.
Terminology and Definitions
This section provides an overview of the terms used in this privacy policy. Many of the terms are drawn from the law and defined mainly in Article 4 GDPR. The legal definitions are binding. The following explanations, on the other hand, are intended above all for the purpose of comprehension. The terms are sorted alphabetically.
